Option B's abstract-startEngine fix preserves the wrong structural choice — the fundamental problem isn't the parent's default implementation, it's that ElectricCar IS-A Car was wrong to begin with for behavior-variation purposes. Option C defends the design by appeal to domain hierarchy, which the article rejects: real-world IS-A doesn't imply OO IS-A when behavior diverges. Option D's factory addition is orthogonal — hiding the choice doesn't fix the structural rigidity; the hybrid problem still exists.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'ElectricCar inheriting Car just to override startEngine with completely different logic isn't sharing — it's coupling. Electric cars don't have engines, so there's no useful gas-engine logic to inherit; the override discards the parent entirely. The fragile-base-class problem is right around the corner: when a hybrid arrives, do I extend Car or ElectricCar? Neither works. The fix is composition: isolate the varying behavior into its own abstraction — Drivetrain, with GasEngine and ElectricMotor implementations — and have CarWithDrivetrain hold one. Hybrids hold two; hydrogen cars get a new Drivetrain; the car class never changes.'
questions.proTip: When a subclass overrides a parent method to provide completely different logic, that's a sign you're using inheritance to model behavior variation — exactly what inheritance is bad at. Default to composition: extract the varying behavior into its own interface and inject it.

questions.question: Two designs use inheritance, but the article calls one a good fit and the other an anti-pattern. Design A: `BankAccount` has `deposit`, `withdraw`, `getBalance` with full implementations; `SavingsAccount extends BankAccount` adds an `interestRate` field; `CheckingAccount extends BankAccount` adds an `overdraftLimit` field. Neither subclass overrides the parent methods. Design B: `Car` has `startEngine` with gas-engine logic; `ElectricCar extends Car` overrides `startEngine` with electric-motor logic. Both designs use the same language feature. What is the precise distinction that makes A a good fit and B an anti-pattern, and why does the article flag the difference?
options: A and B both work; the distinction the article draws is largely stylistic preference. Inheritance can be used either to share or to override, and the choice between them is up to the designer's taste; calling B an anti-pattern is overstated because the override compiles and the runtime behavior is correct.

A is fine because BankAccount has fewer methods than Car, so the coupling cost is smaller; B is an anti-pattern because Car has more methods that ElectricCar would inherit and not use. The distinction is method count: keep parent classes small to keep inheritance safe, and Car was simply too large.

A shares *stable implementation* — deposit, withdraw, and getBalance are identical across all account types, so the parent provides real reuse and the children don't override anything. B uses inheritance to model *behavior variation* — startEngine has fundamentally different logic for gas vs electric, so the parent's implementation isn't shared, only overridden. The article's exact criterion: inheritance is a fit when subclasses 'don't need to override the inherited behavior in ways that break the parent's contract'; B violates that criterion because the override is the entire reason for the subclass.

A is good because Account is a value-object hierarchy and B is bad because Car is a behavior-rich hierarchy; value-object inheritance is safe (data sharing) while behavior-rich inheritance is fragile (logic sharing). The fix for B is to convert Car to a value object first (a struct of properties) and then re-establish the hierarchy at the value level.
questions.explanation: The article's exact distinction: 'Here, the shared implementation is stable and meaningful. Both subclasses genuinely are forms of BankAccount, and they don't need to override the inherited behavior in ways that break the parent's contract. So inheritance is a good fit... The classic mistake made in interviews is using inheritance to model behavior differences. If subclasses need to override methods to provide completely different implementations, that's a sign you're using the wrong tool.' BankAccount works because deposit/withdraw/getBalance are identical across account types — the parent provides real shared implementation. Car/ElectricCar fails because the override is the entire purpose of the subclass; nothing is being shared, only varied. Option A flattens a real distinction. Option B picks a wrong axis (method count) — small parents can still be misused for behavior variation. Option D invents a value-object distinction that's not the article's argument and doesn't apply (BankAccount has methods with logic, not just fields).
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'BankAccount works because deposit, withdraw, and getBalance are identical across all account types — the parent provides real shared implementation, and SavingsAccount and CheckingAccount add data without overriding the methods. Car/ElectricCar fails because the override is the entire reason ElectricCar exists — nothing is being shared, only varied. The distinction the article draws is: inheritance for stable shared implementation is a fit; inheritance for behavior variation is the anti-pattern.'
questions.proTip: Before reaching for inheritance, ask: 'will the subclasses use the parent's methods as-is, or will they override them to do something different?' If the answer is override, you're using inheritance to model variation — composition is almost always a better fit.

questions.question: You decide to model varying car-startup behavior with composition rather than inheritance. You define `class Drivetrain { virtual void start() = 0; }` with `GasEngine` and `ElectricMotor` implementations, and `class Car { Drivetrain& drivetrain; void start() { drivetrain.start(); } }`. Compared to the inheritance-based Car/ElectricCar, what design properties does this composition give you, and what is the trade-off you're explicitly accepting?
options: The composition design lets you model any combination of drivetrains without forcing a Car-vs-ElectricCar choice — hybrids hold two drivetrains, hydrogen cars get a new Drivetrain implementation, and Car itself never changes. You still get polymorphism (drivetrain.start() dispatches to the right concrete type) and abstraction (Car depends on the Drivetrain interface, not on a concrete startup). The trade-off you're accepting is one extra object reference per Car — Car holds a Drivetrain rather than being a more-specific kind of Car — and the slight ceremony of constructing the Drivetrain separately and injecting it; in exchange you lose the parent-child coupling and the fragile-base-class risk that came with the inheritance shape.

The composition design is structurally identical to inheritance — Drivetrain plays the same role as the parent class, and GasEngine/ElectricMotor play the role of subclasses. The only difference is syntactic: composition uses a field reference where inheritance uses a class hierarchy. The trade-off is that composition requires more boilerplate to wire up but produces equivalent runtime behavior, so the choice is mostly stylistic.

The composition design lets you change a car's drivetrain at runtime by reassigning the field, while inheritance fixes the type at construction; this is the major win and is sometimes called 'state-pattern composition.' The trade-off you're accepting is reduced type-system safety — at compile time the Car doesn't know which drivetrain it has, while inheritance pins the type and lets the compiler reason about it.

The composition design gives Car implicit access to all Drivetrain subclasses' methods through the field — calling drivetrain.start() is equivalent to a virtual function call on the parent class. The trade-off you're accepting is heavier memory usage because each Car holds a separate Drivetrain object rather than directly inheriting the data; in performance-critical code this overhead can add up across millions of car instances.
questions.explanation: The article's exact framing: 'Now you can model any kind of car without breaking the hierarchy or overriding logic awkwardly. Want a hybrid? Give it two drivetrains. Want a hydrogen car? Add a new Drivetrain implementation. The CarWithDrivetrain class never changes.' Composition gives you the polymorphism payoff (right drivetrain runs at startup), the abstraction payoff (Car depends on the interface, not the concrete), and the open-ended extensibility (hybrid = two drivetrains; hydrogen = new implementation) without the parent-child coupling and fragile-base-class risk. The cost is the small overhead of holding and wiring a Drivetrain object — one extra reference per Car and the ceremony of separate construction. Option B claims the two are equivalent, which the article explicitly rejects (inheritance creates parent-child coupling that composition avoids). Option C invents a runtime-reassignment benefit that's possible but not the structural argument; the article's framing is about the hybrid-modeling capability, not field reassignment. Option D inverts the comparison — composition with an interface preserves type-system safety identically to inheritance with virtual methods; the cost is operational ceremony, not type safety.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'Composition lets me model hybrids by giving Car two drivetrains, and hydrogen cars by adding a new Drivetrain implementation, without touching Car itself. The polymorphism — drivetrain.start() dispatches to the right concrete type — is preserved; the abstraction — Car depends on the Drivetrain interface — is preserved; what's lost is the parent-child coupling and the fragile-base-class problem. The cost I'm explicitly paying is one extra object reference per Car and the wiring ceremony to construct the drivetrain separately and inject it. That's a small operational cost in exchange for being able to model arbitrary combinations.'
questions.proTip: When you replace inheritance with composition, name what you gained (extensibility, no fragile-base-class) and what you paid (extra reference, wiring ceremony). The trade-off is real and worth saying out loud — that's what proves you're choosing composition rather than just preferring it.

questions.question: Your interviewer says: 'You said inheritance is wrong for Car/ElectricCar, but the existing codebase has thousands of lines that use it. We're going to add a hybrid car next quarter. Walk me through your migration plan.' Two candidates respond. Candidate X: 'Let me design the composition version from scratch and we can migrate later — I'll show you the clean version.' Candidate Y: 'I'd add hybrid support without ripping out the existing inheritance. First step: define a Drivetrain interface and adapt GasEngine and ElectricMotor as new implementations that wrap the existing Car.startEngine and ElectricCar.startEngine logic. Hybrid is a new class that holds two drivetrains and isn't a subclass of either. Existing code keeps working; new code targets the interface. Migration of legacy classes happens incrementally as engineers touch them.' Why does Y's answer score higher despite X's design being structurally cleaner?
options: Y scores higher because they read the interviewer's framing — 'thousands of lines exist' — as a real constraint rather than as background to ignore. Y proposes an incremental migration that adds the new capability (hybrid) immediately while leaving existing code working, which is what real teams do; X's 'let me show you the clean version' answers a different question (greenfield design) and signals X may not have absorbed the constraint. The senior move is to deliver the new capability without a big-bang rewrite, treating the legacy inheritance as an inherited reality to migrate around rather than to delete; the strangler-fig migration pattern handles exactly this case and the article's framing supports it (default to composition for new behavior, keep inheritance only for stable shared implementation that exists today).

X scores higher in most rubrics — clean designs are what interviews are for, and Y's incremental migration sounds slow; the interviewer wants to see your best design and then evaluate whether you can rationalize a migration after. Y's response sounds like they're avoiding committing to a specific design.

Both responses are equivalent — X demonstrates design vision and Y demonstrates migration pragmatism, and the interviewer is testing for either; the difference between them is mostly tactical and the choice depends on how the interviewer frames the follow-up. Most rubrics grade both around the same.

Y scores higher specifically because they invoke the strangler-fig pattern, which is the named industry practice for this exact migration shape; X's response would be acceptable in a startup but not in a senior LLD interview, where pattern-naming for migrations is expected. The decisive factor is naming the strangler-fig pattern.
questions.explanation: The article's framing supports Y's approach implicitly through its prescription: 'For your interview, default to interfaces with composition. Only use inheritance when you genuinely need to share implementation across classes and the relationship is stable. In most LLD interviews, you don't need inheritance at all.' Y honors this for new code (hybrid is composition-based) while leaving existing inheritance in place — because the article doesn't prescribe ripping out existing inheritance that's stable. The migration is incremental: define the Drivetrain interface, adapt existing classes as implementations, route new behavior through the interface, migrate legacy as opportunities arise. X's greenfield-design answer doesn't address the actual constraint (thousands of lines exist) and signals constraint-blindness. Option B inverts the seniority gradient — design vision without constraint-handling is the junior signal; senior signal is delivering the new capability under the real legacy. Option C flattens a real difference. Option D over-emphasizes pattern naming; Y's response is structurally correct without invoking the strangler-fig label, and the article doesn't prescribe pattern naming as the decisive factor.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, when an interviewer flags existing legacy as a constraint, structure your answer around the constraint: 'I'd add the new capability without ripping out the legacy. Define the interface, adapt the legacy as implementations of it, route new code through the interface, and migrate existing classes incrementally as engineers touch them. The hybrid lands next quarter as composition; the existing Car/ElectricCar stay until they're touched for other reasons.' That delivers the capability immediately, respects the legacy reality, and doesn't trade short-term speed for a long-term migration debt.
questions.proTip: When asked to migrate from inheritance to composition under a 'thousands of lines exist' constraint, deliver the new capability through composition immediately, adapt the legacy as a current implementation of the new interface, and migrate the legacy incrementally as it's touched. Big-bang rewrites are the wrong answer to a constraint they refuse to acknowledge.

questions.question: Your team uses the article's approved inheritance shape for accounts: `BankAccount` has full deposit/withdraw/getBalance implementations, and `SavingsAccount`, `CheckingAccount`, and `MoneyMarketAccount` all extend it without overriding. Six months in, a regulatory requirement lands: SavingsAccount's withdraw must enforce a 6-per-month limit (Regulation D historical example); CheckingAccount's withdraw must support overdraft up to a configured limit; MoneyMarketAccount's withdraw must apply a per-withdrawal fee above 3 transactions per month. The team's first instinct is to override withdraw in each subclass. What's the senior critique, and what does this scenario reveal about even the article's approved inheritance shape?
options: Override withdraw in each subclass — that's exactly what inheritance is for, and the regulatory requirement is the kind of subclass-specific behavior the hierarchy was designed to support. The deeper insight is that 'approved' inheritance shapes still benefit from selective overrides; the article's rule against overrides applies only when the override is the *entire reason for the subclass*, which isn't the case here.

The first-instinct override is a fragile-base-class trap. The subclasses started as data-only extensions (interestRate, overdraftLimit) of stable parent behavior — that was the article's approved shape — but the new requirement is *behavior variation*, which is the article's named anti-pattern. The trade-off has inverted: what was inheritance-fit is now composition-fit. The senior fix is to extract the withdraw logic into a WithdrawPolicy interface, give each account a policy via composition, and route BankAccount.withdraw through it. The hierarchy can keep the parent class for data and the few methods that are still stable, or be flattened entirely into account-with-policy if no shared behavior remains. The deeper insight: even an approved inheritance shape becomes wrong when the kind of variation you need flips from data-extension to behavior-variation. The fit isn't a permanent property of the design; it's a function of what the requirements ask of it.

Override withdraw but do it through the Template Method pattern — make BankAccount.withdraw call a protected withdrawCore() and a protected validateWithdraw() that subclasses override. This preserves the inheritance shape, gives subclasses the variation point they need, and is the GoF-canonical answer to 'inheritance with selective behavior variation' in stable hierarchies.

The first instinct is wrong, and the fix is to abandon inheritance entirely — a single Account class with a Type field and a strategy-driven withdraw. The hierarchy was a mistake from the start because regulatory requirements were always coming and account types are inherently behavior-varying; the article's 'approved' shape was a temporary fit that was destined to break, and the senior insight is to anticipate this from day one.
questions.explanation: The trade-off-inversion at L5: a design fit can flip when the kind of variation required changes. The original BankAccount shape was an approved fit because subclasses extended data without overriding methods — stable shared implementation, the article's named-good case. The regulatory requirement converts the situation: each subclass now needs *behavior variation* in withdraw, which is the article's named-bad case. What was inheritance-fit is now composition-fit. The senior move is to recognize the inversion and migrate: extract WithdrawPolicy as an interface, compose each account with a policy, and route the parent's withdraw through it. The hierarchy may keep some shared behavior (deposit, getBalance) and lose withdraw to composition, or be flattened entirely if no shared behavior remains. Why other options tempt: Option A's 'override is fine for selective variation' tempts because the override pattern is widely used in practice, but the article's argument is that override-for-variation creates exactly the fragile-base-class problem you're now setting up — when MoneyMarketAccount's withdraw needs further regulatory variation later, the parent's withdraw becomes increasingly hollow and the subclasses increasingly disconnected from it. Option C's Template Method tempts because it's the GoF formalization of the override-for-variation pattern, but it doesn't escape the underlying coupling — protected hooks are still places where parent and child contracts intertwine, and the regulatory variation can outgrow them. Option D's 'abandon inheritance entirely' overcorrects: the article doesn't say inheritance was wrong from day one, it says the fit was real until the requirements changed. The deeper insight: design fit is contextual to requirements, not a permanent property; senior engineers detect when a fit has inverted and migrate before the cost compounds.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'The original inheritance shape was a fit when subclasses only extended data — interest rate, overdraft limit — and the parent's methods were used as-is. The new regulatory requirement asks for behavior variation per subclass, which is exactly what the article warns against in inheritance. The trade-off has inverted: what was inheritance-fit is now composition-fit. I'd extract WithdrawPolicy as an interface, give each account a policy via composition, and route BankAccount.withdraw through it. Deposit and getBalance can stay in the parent if they're still stable, or the hierarchy can be flattened into account-with-policy. The senior signal is recognizing that fit can change with requirements and migrating before the override pattern compounds into a fragile-base-class problem.'
questions.proTip: Design fit is contextual to requirements, not a permanent property. When the kind of variation a hierarchy needs changes — from data-extension to behavior-variation — the fit can invert. Detect the inversion early and migrate to composition before the override pattern entrenches.

questions.subtopic: Concepts show through how you design
questions.question: Three design situations land in the same interview. (1) Requirements say: 'support email, SMS, and push notifications.' (2) You're sketching a `Cart` class and deciding what to make public. (3) Your draft has `if (user.role == "admin") {...} else if (user.role == "editor") {...} else if (user.role == "viewer") {...}` for permission checks. The candidate, asked to 'apply OOP concepts,' starts naming each one and finding a place to attach it. What's the structural critique, and what's the right framing for treating these three situations?
options: The candidate's approach is fine — naming the concepts and attaching them to design decisions is exactly what 'apply OOP concepts' asks for, and the three situations cleanly map to three concepts (abstraction, encapsulation, polymorphism). The structural critique is that the candidate didn't name inheritance for a fourth attachment, which leaves the canon incomplete.

Each situation has a *cue* that suggests one of the OOP concepts as the right move — but the senior approach isn't to name the concept, it's to make the move and let the concept show through. (1) Multiple notification types is the cue for an interface: define Notifier with send(message), implement Email/SMS/Push; that's abstraction. (2) Designing a class is the cue to default to private fields with methods: Cart owns its items, addItem and removeItem gate mutations; that's encapsulation. (3) Type-checks on a role field is the cue for an interface and dispatch: define Permission with check(action), each role implements it; that's polymorphism. The article's exact framing: 'The concepts show through in how you design, not in what you name.' Naming them while designing is the recitation failure; recognizing the cue and making the structural move is the application that matters.

The candidate's approach is correct in concept but wrong in application — the three situations actually call for two concepts (abstraction and polymorphism), not three; encapsulation isn't applicable to (2) because Cart's items are inherently public-facing in most cart designs. The fix is to drop encapsulation from the analysis and apply abstraction and polymorphism more deeply.

The candidate's approach is fundamentally flawed because OOP concepts aren't attached to design situations at all — they're emergent properties of well-applied SOLID principles, and the right way to handle the three situations is to apply SOLID first (Single Responsibility for the cart, Open/Closed for the notifications, Dependency Inversion for the permissions) and let OOP concepts fall out as consequences.
questions.explanation: The article's closing prescription: 'The concepts show through in how you design, not in what you name. Focus on applying them naturally: Encapsulation: Hide state, expose behavior... Abstraction: Define interfaces for variations... Polymorphism: Let objects handle themselves... Inheritance: Compose behavior, don't inherit it.' Each cue maps to a structural move: multiple-types-of-X → interface; designing-a-class → private fields with methods; type-checks → interface with dispatch. The senior approach is to make the move and let the concept be a *property* of the resulting design, not a label attached to it. Option A normalizes the recitation failure. Option C drops encapsulation incorrectly — the article specifically frames Cart-style designs as encapsulation cues regardless of what's 'public-facing,' since the question is about owning state-changing operations. Option D over-routes through SOLID and inverts the article's framing; the article treats OOP concepts as the implementation mechanisms and SOLID/principles as guidance about when to use them, not the other way around.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, when situations cue OOP concepts, make the move rather than name the concept: 'Multiple notification types — I'll define a Notifier interface with send(message). Cart class — I'll keep items private and expose addItem/removeItem so the cart owns its state. Role-based checks — I'll define a Permission interface with check(action) and each role implements it.' The interviewer hears the design moves and the concepts are visible without being named.
questions.proTip: OOP concepts are diagnostic cues, not labels. When you see a cue (multiple types, designing a class, type-checks), make the structural move. Don't name the concept; let it be a property of the resulting design.

questions.subtopic: Concepts show through how you design
questions.question: Your interviewer ends a parking-lot design with: 'Quick wrap-up — give me a one-line summary of how each of the four OOP concepts shows up in your design, in your own words. No textbook definitions.' What's the strongest answer, and why is the one-liner per concept format what the interviewer is testing for?
options: Encapsulation: ParkingLot's spots are private and the only way to add a vehicle is parkVehicle, so the lot owns how parking happens. Abstraction: Vehicle is an interface that says 'I can tell you my required spot size'; the lot doesn't know if it's a car or a truck. Polymorphism: at runtime, getRequiredSpotSize dispatches to the right subclass, so the lot never branches on type. Inheritance: not used here — composition fit better; I'd reach for inheritance only if a subclass genuinely needed to share parent behavior unchanged.

Encapsulation: keeping fields private and using getters/setters per Java Bean conventions. Abstraction: defining interfaces in front of classes so swap-out is easier. Polymorphism: virtual function dispatch on a class hierarchy. Inheritance: a subclass automatically gets parent behavior. This is the textbook definition for each concept and shows the candidate has the formal vocabulary down.

Encapsulation: SOLID's Single Responsibility Principle, applied at the field level. Abstraction: SOLID's Dependency Inversion Principle, applied at the type level. Polymorphism: the runtime expression of the Open/Closed Principle. Inheritance: a violation of the Composition-over-Inheritance maxim except in cases of stable shared implementation. This grounds each concept in its corresponding SOLID principle for theoretical rigor.

Encapsulation: I picked it because the textbook says to. Abstraction: same reason. Polymorphism: same. Inheritance: I avoided it because the textbook says inheritance is fragile. Honestly, in interviews these labels matter less than the design itself, so I lean on whatever the interviewer wants to hear and adapt.
questions.explanation: The article's closing summary, paraphrased into design-specific one-liners: 'Encapsulation: Hide state, expose behavior... Abstraction: Define interfaces for variations... Polymorphism: Let objects handle themselves... Inheritance: Compose behavior, don't inherit it.' Option A captures each one as it shows up *in this specific design* — what is private, what is interfaced, what dispatches, what was deliberately not used. The interviewer is testing whether the candidate can describe each concept by its concrete effect rather than by formal definition; the one-liner format forces this because there's no room to recite, only room to point at the design move. Option B reverts to textbook definitions, which is exactly what the interviewer asked the candidate not to do. Option C re-routes through SOLID, which is one level removed from the structural moves the question is about. Option D dismisses the question entirely and signals lack of judgment — the interviewer is asking for design-by-effect, not for principle-skepticism.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, when asked for one-liners per concept, point at the design move, not the textbook definition: 'Encapsulation: <what is private here, and what method gates the mutation>. Abstraction: <which interface, and what callers stay ignorant of>. Polymorphism: <where dispatch happens, and what type-check it replaces>. Inheritance: <used here for stable shared behavior, OR deliberately not used because composition fit better>.' Each one-liner names a specific design element and what it buys.
questions.proTip: When asked to summarize OOP concepts in a design, name the *element* (private field X, interface Y, dispatch site Z) and *what it buys*. Avoid textbook definitions — they signal that you can recite, not that you can apply.

questions.subtopic: Concepts show through how you design
questions.question: An interviewer ends a strong design conversation with: 'You never named encapsulation, abstraction, polymorphism, or inheritance during the entire design. Some interviewers would mark you down for that — they want to hear the vocabulary as evidence you understand the concepts. What's your response if the next interviewer in the loop pushes back on that ground?' What is the strongest senior framing of how to handle this consistently across diverse interviewer expectations?
options: Concede on the vocabulary point and start naming the concepts proactively in future interviews — even if it feels like recitation, the cost of saying the words is low and the upside (avoiding markdowns from naming-oriented interviewers) is real. The senior move is to calibrate to the lowest-common-denominator interviewer in any loop.

Defend the design-first approach absolutely — interviewers who grade on vocabulary are applying the wrong rubric, and the senior move is to educate them on why the design itself is the evidence. Push back firmly and trust that genuinely senior interviewers will recognize the difference; capitulating on this point trains the wrong incentive across the industry.

The framing of the question is partially wrong — there isn't a single right answer because the choice isn't between 'always name' and 'never name.' The senior move is to *make the concepts visible by their effects* by default ('items is private so the cart owns mutations,' 'Vehicle is an interface so the lot doesn't branch on type'), and to *also name them on request* without surrendering the design-by-effect framing. When an interviewer asks 'where did you use encapsulation,' the answer points at the design element AND uses the word: 'Encapsulation here — items is private, addItem is the only mutation path, so the cart enforces invariants nothing can bypass.' That answer satisfies naming-oriented interviewers (the word is present) and design-oriented interviewers (the effect is named). It also distinguishes you from candidates who can only do one or the other. The deeper insight: the question forces a false choice; the senior move is to do both, with the effect leading and the label following.

Adapt the strategy by interviewer signal — name concepts in the first 30 seconds of meeting them; if they don't react, drop the naming for the rest of the conversation and shift to design-by-effect. This is a tactical optimization that protects against vocabulary-graders without committing to recitation across the entire round.
questions.explanation: The framing-is-wrong move at L5: the question presents a binary choice (name or don't name) and asks the candidate to pick a side. The senior move is to refuse the binary. The article's prescription — 'concepts show through in how you design, not in what you name' — is guidance about *how* to demonstrate understanding, not a vow of silence. A senior candidate makes the concepts visible by their effects by default ('items is private so the cart owns mutations') and adds the label on request ('encapsulation here — same effect, named'). That answer satisfies both interview rubrics simultaneously: naming-oriented interviewers hear the word; design-oriented interviewers hear the effect. It also distinguishes you from candidates who can only do one or the other. Why other options tempt: Option A's 'concede and recite' protects against the worst-case interviewer at the cost of sounding like a recitation candidate to the best-case interviewer — a net loss across a full loop. Option B's 'defend absolutely' is a principled stance that the article does support, but it forfeits real signal to interviewers who expect vocabulary; principled stances cost real signal when the interviewer's frame isn't wrong, just different. Option D's 'tactical adapt-by-signal' is operationally fragile — reading the interviewer in 30 seconds is hard, the cost of misreading is high, and the strategy makes the candidate inconsistent across interviewers in the same loop, which can be flagged as 'not the same person across rounds.' The deeper insight: in any loop with diverse interviewer expectations, the right strategy is to do both halves of the spectrum simultaneously, not to pick a side.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, when faced with naming-vs-effect tension, do both: lead with the effect ('items is private so the cart owns mutations'), then add the label ('that's encapsulation') if the interviewer is naming-oriented or if the format calls for a wrap-up. The effect is the substance; the label is the courtesy. Doing both simultaneously satisfies divergent interviewer rubrics without sacrificing either signal.
questions.proTip: When asked whether to name OOP concepts in interviews, refuse the binary. Lead with the design effect ('items is private, the cart owns mutations'), follow with the label ('that's encapsulation') if the interviewer is naming-oriented. Both halves cost almost nothing together and signal you can demonstrate concepts and articulate them.
